items.title: Basing Your Weekly Mileage on Your Race Distance? One Coach Explains Why That’s the Wrong Tactic—and the Real Factors to Consider.
Coaches Reveal 8 Signs You’re Running Too Fast on Easy Days
Runners Have Been Climbing the Empire State Building’s 1,576 Steps (the Legal Way) for Decades
The 9 Best Running Headphones to Keep You Motivated

The 8 Best New Balance Running Shoes 2026, for Training, Racing, and More

The Training Plan That Takes You From Walking to Your First Marathon

Run the 2026 TCS New York City Marathon with Runner’s World—3 Guaranteed Entries Are Still Up for Grabs

shoes.name: Saucony Ride
shoes.note: Balanced feel — not too soft, not too firm. Good all-rounder.

shoes.name: New Balance Fresh Foam 880
shoes.note: Comfortable neutral shoe; wide widths available.

shoes.name: Brooks Ghost
shoes.note: Reliable daily trainer — soft, forgiving, great for beginners.

shoes.name: ASICS Gel-Nimbus
shoes.note: Extra cushioning if you want maximum impact protection.

shirts.name: Patagonia Capilene Cool
shirts.note: Great for hot weather and sustainability-minded runners.

shirts.name: Merino base layer (Icebreaker / Smartwool)
shirts.note: Odour-resistant and comfortable for cool-weather runs.

shirts.name: Nike Dri-FIT Miler
shirts.note: Classic beginner top — light, breathable, affordable.

shirts.name: Under Armour Tech 2.0
shirts.note: Soft feel, good value, widely available.

bottoms.name: Lululemon Pace Breaker
bottoms.note: Comfortable liner and phone pocket; popular for everyday runs.

bottoms.name: SOAR Running Split Shorts
bottoms.note: Light and fast — better once you're running regularly.

bottoms.name: Nike Challenger Shorts
bottoms.note: Simple 5" inseam with liner — a safe first pick.

bottoms.name: Tracksmith Session Shorts
bottoms.note: Premium feel with a classic look if you want to invest.

tights.name: 2XU Compression Tights
tights.note: Snug fit — some runners like the supported feel on long runs.

tights.name: Nike Pro Warm Tights
tights.note: Solid cold-weather staple with good warmth-to-weight ratio.

tights.name: Lululemon Fast and Free Tight
tights.note: Multiple pockets and a comfortable high waist.

apple-watch.name: Apple Watch Ultra
apple-watch.note: Overkill for beginners — consider only for trail ultras and multi-day battery needs.

apple-watch.name: Apple Watch SE (2nd gen)
apple-watch.note: Best value — full GPS running features without the premium price.

apple-watch.name: Apple Watch Series 9 / 10
apple-watch.note: Brighter always-on display; easier to read mid-run.

garmin.name: Garmin Forerunner 165
garmin.note: Colour AMOLED screen, beginner-friendly — great first Garmin.

garmin.name: Garmin Forerunner 255 / 265
garmin.note: The sweet spot for half and full marathon training.

garmin.name: Garmin Forerunner 55
garmin.note: Cheapest entry point — basic but reliable GPS tracking.

coros.name: Coros Apex 2
coros.note: Step up for trail runners who want maps and sapphire glass.

coros.name: Coros Pace 3
coros.note: Best beginner pick — light, accurate GPS, weeks of battery.

coros.name: Coros Pace 2
coros.note: Still solid if you find it discounted — slightly older but capable.

strava-app.name: LetsRunNow dashboard
strava-app.note: Connect Strava in your account for streaks, route comparisons, and HR tips.

strava-app.name: Phone-only tracking
strava-app.note: Start here — just open Strava and hit Record before your run.

strava-app.name: Strava + Apple Watch / Garmin / Coros
strava-app.note: Record on your watch; sync automatically when done.

cap.name: Buff UV Headband (winter alternative)
cap.note: Covers ears in cold weather without overheating your head.

cap.name: Smartwool Merino Beanie
cap.note: For freezing temps when a cap isn't enough.

cap.name: Ciele GOCap
cap.note: The cult favourite — light, stylish, great sweat management.

cap.name: Nike Featherlight Cap
cap.note: Budget-friendly and does the job well.

goggles.name: Oakley Sutro Lite
goggles.note: Premium option with excellent clarity and stability.

goggles.name: Julbo Fury
goggles.note: Great for trail runners who want wraparound coverage.

goggles.name: Goodr
goggles.note: Affordable, fun colours, no-slip fit — perfect starter shades.

sunscreen.name: Supergoop! Play Everyday Lotion SPF 50
sunscreen.note: Light feel, no white cast — good for daily training.

sunscreen.name: Sun Bum Original SPF 50
sunscreen.note: Popular sport formula with a pleasant scent; reapply on long runs.

sunscreen.name: Neutrogena Sport Face SPF 70
sunscreen.note: Sweat-resistant and less likely to sting eyes than body formulas.

sunscreen.name: Banana Boat Sport Ultra SPF 50
sunscreen.note: Budget-friendly, widely available, solid for arms and legs.

chafing-creams.name: Bandelettes or Nip Guards
chafing-creams.note: Physical barriers for nipple chafing on long runs.

chafing-creams.name: Vaseline
chafing-creams.note: Cheap and works in a pinch — not ideal for fabric stains.

chafing-creams.name: Body Glide
chafing-creams.note: The classic stick — apply to thighs, feet, and anywhere that rubs.

chafing-creams.name: Squirrel's Nut Butter
chafing-creams.note: Natural anti-chafe balm; popular with trail runners.

hydration-tablets.name: Precision Fuel & Hydration
hydration-tablets.note: Customizable sodium levels to match your sweat rate.

hydration-tablets.name: SaltStick FastChews
hydration-tablets.note: Chewable electrolytes if you prefer not to mix drinks.

hydration-tablets.name: Nuun Sport
hydration-tablets.note: Light, fizzy, easy on the stomach — great starter electrolyte.

hydration-tablets.name: LMNT
hydration-tablets.note: Higher sodium — popular for heavy sweaters and hot climates.

gels.name: Huma Gel
gels.note: Made with chia and fruit — good if standard gels upset your gut.

gels.name: Spring Energy
gels.note: Whole-food style fuel for runners who want real-ingredient options.

gels.name: Maurten Gel 100
gels.note: Gentle on the stomach — popular with marathoners.

gels.name: GU Energy Gel
gels.note: Wide flavour range; easy to find at races and shops.

hydration-packs.name: Osprey Dyna LT
hydration-packs.note: Hybrid pack/vest — great for trail beginners.

hydration-packs.name: FlipBelt or handheld bottle
hydration-packs.note: Simpler option for shorter long runs — no vest needed yet.

hydration-packs.name: Salomon Active Skin 8
hydration-packs.note: Light race vest — popular for half and full marathon training.

hydration-packs.name: Nathan VaporHowe 2.0
hydration-packs.note: Comfortable women's-specific fit with good storage.

shoes.name: New Balance Fresh Foam 880 (wide)
shoes.note: Extra forefoot room if standard women's widths feel tight.

shoes.name: Hoka Clifton (women's)
shoes.note: Plush cushioning with a roomy toe box — good for longer easy miles.

shoes.name: Brooks Ghost (women's)
shoes.note: Same forgiving ride with a women's-specific last — try both standard and wide.

shoes.name: ASICS Gel-Cumulus (women's)
shoes.note: Soft daily trainer; popular for higher arches and narrower heels.

shirts.name: Nike Dri-FIT Swoosh Women's Tank
shirts.note: Light, breezy top with a flattering cut — easy first technical shirt.

shirts.name: Oiselle Flyout Tank
shirts.note: Women's running brand — soft fabric and a relaxed race-day feel.

shirts.name: Brooks Drive 3 Pocket Run Bra
shirts.note: High-support sports bra with a phone pocket — reduces bounce on easy and long runs.

shirts.name: Lululemon Energy Longline Bra
shirts.note: Medium support for smaller frames; comfortable under tanks.

bottoms.name: Athleta Ultimate Lined Short
bottoms.note: Mid-rise option with a secure waistband for longer runs.

bottoms.name: Nike Tempo Women's Short
bottoms.note: Classic curved-hem running short — widely available and affordable.

bottoms.name: Lululemon Hotty Hot High-Rise
bottoms.note: Light 4" short with liner and pockets — a common women's starter pick.

bottoms.name: Oiselle Roga Short
bottoms.note: Women's-specific cut with a comfortable brief liner.

tights.name: Sweaty Betty Power Leggings
tights.note: Comfortable compression feel without feeling restrictive.

tights.name: Brooks Method 7/8 Tight
tights.note: Running-specific fit with reflective details for dawn and dusk.

tights.name: Lululemon Fast and Free High-Rise Tight
tights.note: High waist stays put; multiple pockets for phone and gels.

tights.name: Athleta Ultimate Stash Tight
tights.note: Soft, squat-proof fabric with side pockets — great for cold commutes.

apple-watch.name: Sport Loop or Solo Loop (small)
apple-watch.note: Flexible bands that fit smaller wrists without extra tail flapping.

apple-watch.name: Apple Watch SE (40mm)
apple-watch.note: Smaller case fits narrower wrists without overpowering your arm.

apple-watch.name: Apple Watch Series 9 / 10 (41mm)
apple-watch.note: Compact size with a brighter screen for glancing at pace mid-run.

garmin.name: Garmin Forerunner 165
garmin.note: 43mm case suits smaller wrists; quick-release bands for a secure fit.

garmin.name: Garmin Forerunner 265S
garmin.note: Smaller "S" size — same features with a narrower case and band.

garmin.name: Garmin silicone band (small)
garmin.note: Stock bands run long — size down so the watch doesn't slide on sweaty runs.

coros.name: Coros Apex 2 (42mm)
coros.note: Smaller case option if you want maps without a bulky watch face.

coros.name: Coros Pace 3
coros.note: Light 30g case works well on smaller wrists — silicone band in S/M.

coros.name: Coros Pace 3 nylon band
coros.note: Softer, lower-profile strap if the stock band feels stiff or long.

strava-app.name: Strava + sports watch (40–43mm)
strava-app.note: Smaller watches pair the same way — record on wrist, review on phone after.

strava-app.name: FlipBelt or armband (phone carry)
strava-app.note: If you run phone-only, waist belts often sit better over high-rise shorts than pockets.

strava-app.name: Strava privacy zones
strava-app.note: Hide start/end near home — useful if you often run solo from the same address.

cap.name: Buff Coolnet UV+ Headband
cap.note: Keeps hair off your face on hot runs without full hat hair.

cap.name: Smartwool Merino Headband
cap.note: Covers ears in winter while leaving room for a ponytail or braid.

cap.name: Ciele TRKCap
cap.note: Ponytail-friendly opening in back — no awkward bun stuffing.

cap.name: Nike AeroBill Featherlight (women's)
cap.note: Light cap with a lower profile that fits smaller head sizes.

goggles.name: Oakley Revolver
goggles.note: Women's-specific fit with secure temples for sweaty miles.

goggles.name: 100% Speedcraft SL (narrow)
goggles.note: Lightweight with a slimmer temple if standard sport frames feel wide.

goggles.name: Goodr Circle G
goggles.note: Smaller frame and nose bridge — fits narrower faces without sliding.

goggles.name: Tifosi Swank (XS)
goggles.note: Extra-small size option for petite faces.

sunscreen.name: Neutrogena Sport Face Oil-Free SPF 70
sunscreen.note: Sweat-resistant without a greasy feel on forehead and shoulders.

sunscreen.name: Banana Boat Light as Air SPF 50
sunscreen.note: Thin body lotion for arms and legs — absorbs quickly before you lace up.

sunscreen.name: EltaMD UV Clear SPF 46
sunscreen.note: Lightweight face formula — popular under ponytails and visors.

sunscreen.name: Supergoop! Unseen Sunscreen SPF 40
sunscreen.note: Invisible finish that won't clash with sports bra straps or tank lines.

chafing-creams.name: Bandelettes thigh bands
chafing-creams.note: Lace-free barrier when shorts liners aren't enough for thigh rub.

chafing-creams.name: Monistat Chafing Relief Powder Gel
chafing-creams.note: Drugstore option many runners use along bra lines and inner thighs.

chafing-creams.name: Body Glide (sports bra band & underarms)
chafing-creams.note: Apply along bra band, straps, and underarm seams before long runs.

chafing-creams.name: Shefit Ultimate Sports Bra
chafing-creams.note: Adjustable zip-front bra — dial in support to cut bounce-related rubbing.

hydration-tablets.name: SaltStick FastChews
hydration-tablets.note: Easy to dose on long runs without carrying a full bottle mix.

hydration-tablets.name: LMNT (half packet in 16 oz)
hydration-tablets.note: High sodium for heavy sweaters — start with a lighter mix if new to electrolytes.

hydration-tablets.name: Nuun Sport (lower-sugar flavours)
hydration-tablets.note: Gentle on the stomach — useful if gels and sweet drinks feel heavy.

hydration-tablets.name: Precision Fuel PF 30 Electrolyte
hydration-tablets.note: Sweat-test kits help match sodium needs across cycle phases.

gels.name: GU Roctane (caffeine-free flavours)
gels.note: Test caffeine-free first if you're sensitive during your cycle.

gels.name: Spring Energy Canaberry
gels.note: Mild, fruit-forward taste — good starter gel for long runs.

gels.name: Huma Gel
gels.note: Real-food ingredients — often easier on sensitive stomachs.

gels.name: Maurten Gel 100
gels.note: Lower osmolality can sit better during hard efforts.

hydration-packs.name: Salomon Active Skin 8 (S/M women's)
hydration-packs.note: Race vest with front flasks — check size chart for bust fit.

hydration-packs.name: FlipBelt
hydration-packs.note: Waist belt option if vests chafe under sports bra bands.

hydration-packs.name: Nathan VaporHowe 2.0
hydration-packs.note: Women's-specific cut — shorter torso and shaped sternum straps.

hydration-packs.name: Osprey Dyna 15 (women's)
hydration-packs.note: Hydration pack sized for narrower shoulders and shorter backs.
